title: Tier tagging + metrics propagation plan (Hosted vs Dedicated) — SLO/Error Budget program
preview:
Context
- We’re rolling out tiered SLOs + error budgets for Hosted (shared) vs Dedicated (reserved capacity). For those to be credible, tier labeling must be:
  (1) set once (authoritative),
  (2) propagated end-to-end across gateway → routing/control plane → model serving,
  (3) present on the right metrics/logs/traces used for SLIs, and
  (4) safe from cardinality blow-ups.
- This doc is the concrete implementation plan for “tier tagging” in telemetry. It’s intended as the working plan used by Telemetry + Gateway + Routing to land ENG-4922 and unblock dashboards/alerts.

Goals
1) Canonical tags: define the minimal label set required to compute SLIs for availability, latency, and streaming reliability by tier/region/endpoint class.
2) Authoritative source: tier tag set at ingress (API gateway), not “inferred later” in routing.
3) Propagation: tier tag carried in request context and applied consistently to:
   - metrics emitted at gateway
   - metrics emitted in routing/control plane
   - metrics emitted in model serving request path
   - distributed traces and structured logs (for debug/attribution)
4) Cardinality guardrails: avoid per-customer/per-model/per-request labeling on high-volume metrics. Provide a controlled path for Dedicated account-level SLO dashboards.
5) Validation and rollout: tier tag coverage checks (per endpoint/tier/region), staged rollout (shadow labels → dashboards → alerting → enforcement).

Non-goals (for this phase)
- Private deployment tier tagging (we will keep a placeholder label value; enforcement is not in scope this quarter).
- Full per-customer SLO reporting for Hosted (we will not do account_id tags on high-volume Hosted metrics).

Canonical taxonomy (what tags we will use)
Required tags (must exist on all SLI-bearing metrics)
- deployment_tier: one of {hosted, dedicated, private_unknown}
  - Notes: “private_unknown” is a placeholder for future; do NOT treat it as a tier with SLO enforcement.
- region: cloud region identifier (e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-1)
- endpoint_class: one of {chat, completions, embeddings, rerank}
  - Notes: keep this coarse to limit series count.
- transport: one of {http, sse}
  - sse used for streaming/chat stream where disconnect accounting matters.

Recommended tags (allowed where series volume is bounded)
- route_group: stable, low-cardinality logical route (e.g., “v1_chat_completions”, “v1_embeddings”). Do not use raw URL path.
- model_family: coarse bucket (e.g., “llama3”, “mixtral”, “qwen2”) rather than model_id.
- status_class: {2xx, 4xx, 5xx, timeout, cancel}
  - Note: status_class is not needed on latency histograms if we track success latency separately.

Strictly prohibited on high-volume metrics
- request_id, trace_id
- raw_model_id / model_version (unless pre-bucketed)
- customer_id / account_id for Hosted
- prompt tokens / completion tokens as labels (use exemplars or logs)

Dedicated account tagging (special case)
- dedicated_account_slug: allowed only on Dedicated SLO “thin” metrics with controlled sampling OR on derived/recording-rule metrics created from low-cardinality source series.
  - We will not add dedicated_account_slug to gateway request counters by default.
  - Proposed compromise:
    - Base SLIs: aggregate by deployment_tier only.
    - Dedicated customer dashboards: computed via dedicated control-plane mapping + query-time filtering of dedicated clusters / namespaces, not per-request account_id labels.
  - If Product/Sales requires per-account SLO numbers, we should generate them as scheduled jobs (budget computation pipeline) rather than raw Prometheus series explosion.

Where tags are set (source of truth)
A) API Gateway (ingress) — authoritative tier assignment
- Set deployment_tier at the earliest point we can reliably identify:
  - Dedicated: request resolved to a Dedicated environment via auth/API key mapping (control plane lookup or cached map). Result must be deterministic.
  - Hosted: default for all other valid authenticated requests.
  - Invalid/unauthenticated requests: still labeled (deployment_tier=hosted) but excluded in SLI computation via SLI filters (see below).
- Region is known at gateway instance level.
- endpoint_class and transport derived from route handler and response mode (SSE vs HTTP).

B) Request context propagation
- The gateway attaches a “tier context” object to the internal request envelope forwarded to routing/control plane:
  - deployment_tier
  - tier_source: {auth_map, header_override, default}
  - dedicated_cluster_id (Dedicated only; internal id, not customer identifier)
- The context is propagated:
  - through routing policy evaluation
  - into model serving request metadata
  - into trace attributes
  - into log fields
- Key requirement: fallback/reroute must preserve original deployment_tier. (Routing may change model backend/region, but tier stays the customer contract tier.)

C) Routing/control plane
- Must *not* re-derive tier. Must accept tier from context and pass through.
- Emits routing decision metrics with deployment_tier and region:
  - route decision counts
  - fallback events
  - overload shed events

D) Model serving path
- Serving runtime emits request/latency metrics keyed by deployment_tier + region + endpoint_class + transport.
- Avoid per-model/per-replica labels. If we need “top contributors,” we should do it via logs + traces and sampled metrics.

How tags appear in telemetry
1) Metrics (Prometheus-style; naming illustrative)
Gateway
- redwood_api_requests_total{deployment_tier, region, endpoint_class, transport, status_class}
- redwood_api_request_duration_seconds_bucket{deployment_tier, region, endpoint_class, transport}
- redwood_stream_disconnects_total{deployment_tier, region, endpoint_class="chat", transport="sse", disconnect_reason}

Routing/control plane
- redwood_routing_decisions_total{deployment_tier, region, decision="primary|fallback|reroute"}
- redwood_overload_shed_total{deployment_tier, region, shed_reason}
- redwood_policy_eval_latency_seconds_bucket{deployment_tier, region}

Serving
- redwood_model_requests_total{deployment_tier, region, endpoint_class, status_class}
- redwood_model_request_duration_seconds_bucket{deployment_tier, region, endpoint_class}
- redwood_kv_cache_hit_total{deployment_tier, region}  (optional; keep low-cardinality)

2) Traces
- Trace root span (gateway) must include attributes:
  - deployment_tier
  - region
  - endpoint_class
  - transport
  - dedicated_cluster_id (Dedicated only; internal)
- Downstream spans should inherit deployment_tier and region automatically (propagation).

3) Logs
- Structured log fields on gateway + routing + serving:
  - deployment_tier
  - region
  - endpoint_class
  - transport
  - decision / fallback flags (routing)
- Avoid including account_id in logs unless already part of auth context and access controlled. If present, ensure it’s redacted/hashed per logging policy.

SLI accounting rules (how tags + filters combine)
Availability / success rate SLI
- Numerator: successful requests
- Denominator: valid requests
- Valid request definition (initial proposal; align with SLI spec doc):
  - Include: all requests reaching gateway handler with valid auth and passing basic request validation
  - Exclude: 4xx due to client errors (invalid request schema, unsupported params), rate limited (429), and explicit client aborts
  - Include in “error”: 5xx, gateway timeouts, upstream timeouts, serving errors, stream disconnects attributable to server

- Compute latency histograms on successful requests only (to avoid “fast failures” hiding tail latency).
- Use end-to-end timing boundary:
  - Start: gateway request received
  - Stop:
    - non-streaming: response headers committed
    - streaming (SSE): time-to-first-token (TTFT) and stream duration tracked separately

- Define stream failure as server-side disconnect/error prior to “normal completion.”
- Track reasons:
  - server_error
  - upstream_reset
  - gateway_timeout
  - client_abort (excluded from SLI)
- Ensure disconnect_reason is coarse and bounded.

- Client retries: counted as separate requests (normal).

  - Keep request_id constant in tracing only; do NOT emit extra request_total increments for internal retries unless explicitly part of SLI definition.

- Shed responses (e.g., 503 due to overload): count against availability SLI unless we explicitly classify and exclude (current direction: count against SLO; shedding is a last resort).

Cardinality guardrails (hard rules)
- Required tags only on request_total and latency histograms.
- Any metric that includes route_group or model_family must be reviewed by Thomas Baird (metrics cardinality control) before merge.
- Any tag that can exceed 1k unique values/day is assumed prohibited unless it is:
  - on sampled metrics (and sampling rate is stated), OR
  - produced by a batch job / recording rule with bounded series.

  - availability success rate (rolling 30d)
  - latency p50/p95/p99 (rolling 1h/6h/24h)
  - streaming reliability (disconnect rate)
- Drill-down panels:
  - fallback rate
  - overload shed rate
  - routing policy eval latency

  - dedicated_cluster_id scoping (control-plane mapping) and/or namespace scoping

- Avoid account_id label. If absolutely required, generate per-account SLO snapshots via the budget computation job and store in a separate datastore (not raw Prometheus).

- Align with ADR-0142: tier tagging at ingress.

Phase 1 — Gateway instrumentation (ENG-4922 / PR-28411)
- Add deployment_tier label to gateway request and latency metrics.
- Add transport + endpoint_class labels.
- Add streaming disconnect metric with disconnect_reason.
- Add trace/log fields.
- Add a “tier_source” debug label to logs/traces only (NOT metrics).

Phase 2 — Routing + serving propagation
- Update internal request envelope schema to include tier context.
- Ensure routing policy engine passes through tier.
- Add deployment_tier label to routing decision metrics.
- Add deployment_tier label to serving request/latency metrics.

Phase 3 — Recording rules + coverage validation (ENG-4926)
- Create recording rules that normalize label sets (to protect queries from inconsistent labels):
  - slo:requests_total
  - slo:errors_total
  - slo:latency_bucket
  - slo:stream_disconnects_total
- Add a CI check that for each (deployment_tier, region, endpoint_class) in the SLO config, the required recording rules have non-empty series.

Phase 4 — Dashboards (observability-pack PR-6312)
- Build tiered SLO dashboards off recording rules only.
- Add annotations for deploys/incidents.

Phase 5 — Alerting + enforcement dependencies
- Enable burn-rate alerts once tier tag coverage is >99% for 7 consecutive days.
- Deploy gates depend on “single source of truth” for budget state; metric gaps must block enforcement rollout.

Rollout checklist / validation
- Data quality checks (daily for first 2 weeks):
  - % of gateway requests with deployment_tier present (target: 100%)
  - % of serving requests with deployment_tier present (target: >99%; allow a small initial tail)
  - Label consistency: deployment_tier values limited to allowed set
  - Ratio sanity: gateway requests vs serving requests by tier (expected within bounded delta; investigate drift)
- Backfill:
  - We will not backfill raw tiered labels historically. SLO windows start once instrumentation is in place.

Open questions / decisions needed
1) Dedicated per-account SLO reporting:
   - Do we commit to an account-level view in this quarter or keep it as “Dedicated aggregate” + per-cluster debug views?
   - If required: prefer batch SLO snapshots over account_id labels.
2) Endpoint_class classification:
   - Confirm mapping for new endpoints (rerank, tools/function calling routes) to avoid “unknown” drift.
3) Streaming SLI definition:
   - Finalize which disconnect_reason values are “server attributable” vs client abort.
4) Region labeling when cross-region fallback occurs:
   - For SLO reporting, should region be request ingress region or serving region?
   - Current recommendation: region = ingress region (what customer experiences); add serving_region only to logs/traces, not metrics.

Notes to self / reminders
- Biggest risk remains cardinality. Any “just add account_id/model_id label” proposals should be pushed into logs/traces or offline aggregations.
- If tier_source needs debugging, keep it out of Prometheus labels.
- Make sure tier tag is applied before any early returns at gateway (auth failures, validation errors) so exclusions can be computed consistently.

Owner next steps (Logan)
- Schedule 30m review with Gateway (Amara) + Routing (Mina/Ravi) + Metrics cardinality (Thomas) to confirm label set and the “no account_id label” stance.
- Add coverage validation to slo-toolkit (or existing telemetry validation) so we catch gaps before alerting turns on.

source.title: Audit Exporter dashboards + metrics inventory (beta)
source.content:
Context
- SIEM-ready audit export beta: exporter service pushes audit events to Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs intake, and S3 delivery.
- This doc is a spreadsheet-like inventory of proposed metrics and dashboard panels.
- Primary goals:
  1) measure export lag + delivery success, per adapter type
  2) surface customer-impacting failures (auth, throttling, misconfig)
  3) keep label/cardinality safe for shared hosted and dedicated environments

Metric conventions (proposed)
- Prefix: audit_exporter_
- Use Prometheus-style suffixes: _total counters, _seconds for time, _bytes, _ratio, _info for build/version.
- Avoid embedding identifiers in metric names.

Labeling + cardinality rules (beta)
- Allowed low-card labels:
  - region (e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-1)
  - deployment_mode (hosted|dedicated)  [NOTE: private out of scope for beta]
  - adapter (s3|splunk_hec|datadog_logs)
  - outcome (success|error|dropped|skipped)
  - error_class (auth|throttle|network|timeout|bad_request|server_error|unknown)
  - http_status (optional; bucket into {2xx,4xx,5xx} preferred over full code)
  - reason (small controlled vocabulary only)
  - pause_reason (manual|max_lag|backpressure|circuit_breaker)
- Avoid / prohibit high-card labels:
  - tenant_id, workspace_id, user_id, destination_id, config_id, request_id, event_id
  - raw endpoint/host, full URL, Splunk index/sourcetype, S3 bucket name, Datadog tags
- If per-destination visibility is required:
  - Prefer sampling via logs/traces or a separate internal-only, rate-limited “status endpoint” query.
  - If we must label destinations, use destination_scope=beta_cohort and destination_hash (short, stable, low volume) behind an internal metrics pipeline, not default production Prometheus.

Definitions
- Export lag: time between audit event.created_at and successful delivery acknowledgement at destination adapter.
- Checkpoint age: how “stale” the exporter’s persisted checkpoint is (wall time since last checkpoint write).
- Delivery success rate: successful batches / attempted batches (rolling window), computed at dashboard level.

Inventory table (v0.9)
Columns: Metric | Type | Unit | Description | Labels (allowed) | Cardinality risk | Dashboard panels | Notes / Implementation

1) audit_exporter_export_lag_seconds
- Type: histogram
- Unit: seconds
- Description: End-to-end lag from event timestamp to destination ack (measured on successful delivery only).
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Export lag p50/p90/p99; Lag burn-down
- Notes: Ensure histogram buckets cover 1s -> 24h (beta outages can create multi-hour lag). Proposed buckets: 1,2,5,10,30,60,120,300,600,1800,3600,21600,86400.

2) audit_exporter_lag_max_seconds
- Type: gauge
- Unit: seconds
- Description: Current max observed lag in last scrape interval (or last N minutes) per adapter.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: “Max lag” single-stat per adapter; paging threshold overlay
- Notes: Useful for alerts; avoids heavy quantile queries if histograms are expensive.

3) audit_exporter_events_received_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: events
- Description: Audit events pulled/consumed by exporter before filtering.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Ingest rate; compare to exported
- Notes: Source may be queue/stream consumer; doesn’t mean eligible for export.

4) audit_exporter_events_exported_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: events
- Description: Audit events successfully exported (acked by adapter).
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Export throughput; success ratio vs received
- Notes: Count on ack, not on send.

5) audit_exporter_events_dropped_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: events
- Description: Events dropped by filtering engine or redaction policy enforcement.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, reason
- Cardinality risk: Medium (reason must be controlled)
- Dashboard panels: Dropped events over time; top drop reasons
- Notes: reason vocabulary (beta): filter_no_match | pii_policy_violation | malformed_event | schema_version_unsupported.

6) audit_exporter_batches_attempted_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: batches
- Description: Number of batches attempted to a destination adapter.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Delivery attempts; denominator for success rate
- Notes: Batch definition differs per adapter (Splunk payload, Datadog intake payload, S3 object write).

7) audit_exporter_batches_succeeded_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: batches
- Description: Batches successfully delivered and checkpointed.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Delivery success rate; success trend
- Notes: Increment only after checkpoint commit to maintain “at-least-once” semantics.

8) audit_exporter_batch_size_events
- Type: histogram
- Unit: events
- Description: Distribution of batch sizes (events per batch).
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Batch size p50/p90; tuning view
- Notes: Helps tune rate limiting/backpressure and payload sizing.

9) audit_exporter_adapter_request_duration_seconds
- Type: histogram
- Unit: seconds
- Description: Adapter end-to-end request time (includes retries? preferred: per attempt).
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter, outcome
- Cardinality risk: Medium
- Dashboard panels: Adapter latency p95; correlation with lag spikes
- Notes: outcome vocabulary: success|error|timeout.

10) audit_exporter_adapter_requests_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: requests
- Description: Total adapter send attempts (including retries).
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter, outcome
- Cardinality risk: Medium
- Dashboard panels: Requests/sec; error rate overlay
- Notes: outcome values should be low-card only.

11) audit_exporter_adapter_errors_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: errors
- Description: Adapter errors by class.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter, error_class
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Error breakdown by class; “auth errors” single-stat
- Notes: error_class mapping guidance:
  - Splunk 401/403 => auth
  - Splunk 429 => throttle
  - Datadog 403 => auth (often key scope)
  - network/DNS => network
  - request timeouts => timeout
  - 4xx non-auth/throttle => bad_request
  - 5xx => server_error

12) audit_exporter_retry_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: retries
- Description: Count of retries performed by exporter, by reason.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter, reason
- Cardinality risk: Medium (reason must be controlled)
- Dashboard panels: Retries/min; retry reason distribution
- Notes: reason vocabulary: throttle | transient_network | timeout | server_error.

13) audit_exporter_backoff_sleep_seconds_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: seconds
- Description: Total time spent sleeping due to backoff (proxy for downstream pain).
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Backoff time trend; correlate with lag
- Notes: If hard to implement, drop for beta.

14) audit_exporter_dlq_messages_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: messages/batches
- Description: Messages/batches moved to dead-letter queue.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter, error_class
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: DLQ volume; DLQ by adapter
- Notes: Should page only when sustained + customer impact.

15) audit_exporter_dlq_depth
- Type: gauge
- Unit: messages/batches
- Description: Current DLQ depth.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: DLQ depth (sparkline); “needs replay” indicator
- Notes: Depth gauge depends on DLQ backing store.

16) audit_exporter_checkpoint_age_seconds
- Type: gauge
- Unit: seconds
- Description: Wall-clock age since last successful checkpoint write.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Checkpoint staleness; “stuck exporter” detector
- Notes: Useful even when lag histogram is sparse.

17) audit_exporter_config_sync_success_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: syncs
- Description: Successful config syncs from control plane.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Config sync success; sync rate
- Notes: Exporter can appear “healthy” but misconfigured; sync signals help.

18) audit_exporter_config_sync_errors_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: errors
- Description: Config sync failures.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, error_class
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Config sync errors; error class breakdown
- Notes: error_class here: auth (to control plane), network, server_error, bad_config.

19) audit_exporter_active_configs
- Type: gauge
- Unit: configs
- Description: Number of active export configurations currently loaded.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Active exports count; compare with beta cohort list
- Notes: Avoid per-tenant breakdown.

20) audit_exporter_paused
- Type: gauge
- Unit: boolean (0/1)
- Description: Whether exporter auto-paused deliveries.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter, pause_reason
- Cardinality risk: Medium
- Dashboard panels: Pause state; pause reason breakdown
- Notes: pause_reason must be small. This is the “what to do” signal for on-call.

21) audit_exporter_queue_depth
- Type: gauge
- Unit: messages
- Description: Depth of internal queue (or consumer lag) for audit events.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Backpressure view; queue depth vs exported rate
- Notes: Implementation depends on queueing model; may be split by stage.

22) audit_exporter_filter_eval_duration_seconds
- Type: histogram
- Unit: seconds
- Description: Time spent evaluating export filters per event or per batch.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Filter performance; regression watch
- Notes: Useful for catching pathological filters; ensure it’s not per-tenant labeled.

23) audit_exporter_build_info
- Type: gauge
- Unit: info
- Description: Build/version info for exporter.
- Labels: version, git_sha, region, deployment_mode
- Cardinality risk: Low (but ensure version/sha are not per pod)
- Dashboard panels: Deploy version table
- Notes: Standardize with existing build_info patterns.

Dashboard panel mapping (Grafana)
Dashboard: “Audit Exporter - Overview (Beta)”
- Row: SLO / Customer impact
  - Export lag (p50/p90/p99) by adapter
  - Max lag by adapter (single-stat)
  - Delivery success rate (batches_succeeded_total / batches_attempted_total) by adapter
  - Pause state (paused=1) with pause_reason
- Row: Throughput + volume
  - Events received/sec vs exported/sec
  - Batch size distribution by adapter
- Row: Errors + retries
  - Adapter errors rate by error_class
  - Retries/min by adapter + reason
  - Backoff sleep seconds (if shipped)
- Row: Reliability tooling
  - DLQ depth + DLQ messages/sec
  - Checkpoint age
  - Config sync errors
- Row: Performance
  - Adapter request duration p95 by adapter + outcome
  - Filter eval duration p95

Dashboard: “Audit Exporter - Adapter deep dive”
- Variables: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Panels:
  - Requests/sec + error rate
  - Request duration heatmap
  - Errors by class
  - Retries by reason
  - Correlate lag p95 to retries

Alert candidates (align to SLO doc; draft)
- Page (severity: high):
  - Max lag > X minutes for Y minutes (by adapter), where X/Y per beta SLO (Sean/Aisha to confirm)
  - paused=1 sustained > 10m (any adapter) AND events_received_total increasing
- Ticket (severity: medium):
  - adapter_errors_total{error_class="auth"} increasing (auth failures usually need customer action)
  - dlq_depth > 0 for > 30m (beta); create ticket with runbook link
  - config_sync_errors_total increasing for > 15m

Open questions / TODO
- Do we need a “customer visible” vs “internal only” metrics split? Current assumption: everything above is internal.
- Should we compute lag based on audit event timestamp (created_at) vs emitter timestamp (emitted_at) vs exporter receive time? (My preference: created_at; Security to confirm semantics.)
- Splunk HEC: do we want a metric for 429 rate specifically? (Could be error_class=throttle; avoid http_status label.)
- Datadog: 403 key scoping confusion recurring—should we special-case a “dd_key_scope_invalid” reason? (If yes, keep as reason value under auth.)
- S3: do we need multipart upload metrics? Likely too detailed for beta; use adapter request duration + errors.

Notes / references
- Implementation started in audit-log-exporter PR-1098 (metrics scaffolding). Ensure names stay stable; changing metrics breaks dashboards and alert rules.
- Dashboard + alerts shipping tracked in observability-pack PR-392.
- Please keep this doc updated as we learn from beta incidents (e.g., downstream throttling, backpressure tuning).

  - Correlate lag p95 to retries

Alert candidates (align to SLO doc; draft)
- Page (severity: high):
  - Max lag > X minutes for Y minutes (by adapter), where X/Y per beta SLO (Sean/Aisha to confirm)
  - paused=1 sustained > 10m (any adapter) AND events_received_total increasing
- Ticket (severity: medium):
  - adapter_errors_total{error_class="auth"} increasing (auth failures usually need customer action)
  - dlq_depth > 0 for > 30m (beta); create ticket with runbook link
  - config_sync_errors_total increasing for > 15m

Open questions / TODO
- Do we need a “customer visible” vs “internal only” metrics split? Current assumption: everything above is internal.
- Should we compute lag based on audit event timestamp (created_at) vs emitter timestamp (emitted_at) vs exporter receive time? (My preference: created_at; Security to confirm semantics.)
- Splunk HEC: do we want a metric for 429 rate specifically? (Could be error_class=throttle; avoid http_status label.)
- Datadog: 403 key scoping confusion recurring—should we special-case a “dd_key_scope_invalid” reason? (If yes, keep as reason value under auth.)
- S3: do we need multipart upload metrics? Likely too detailed for beta; use adapter request duration + errors.

Notes / references
- Implementation started in audit-log-exporter PR-1098 (metrics scaffolding). Ensure names stay stable; changing metrics breaks dashboards and alert rules.
- Dashboard + alerts shipping tracked in observability-pack PR-392.
- Please keep this doc updated as we learn from beta incidents (e.g., downstream throttling, backpressure tuning).
text:
Context
- SIEM-ready audit export beta: exporter service pushes audit events to Splunk HEC, Datadog Logs intake, and S3 delivery.
- This doc is a spreadsheet-like inventory of proposed metrics and dashboard panels.
- Primary goals:
  1) measure export lag + delivery success, per adapter type
  2) surface customer-impacting failures (auth, throttling, misconfig)
  3) keep label/cardinality safe for shared hosted and dedicated environments

Metric conventions (proposed)
- Prefix: audit_exporter_
- Use Prometheus-style suffixes: _total counters, _seconds for time, _bytes, _ratio, _info for build/version.
- Avoid embedding identifiers in metric names.

Labeling + cardinality rules (beta)
- Allowed low-card labels:
  - region (e.g., us-east-1, eu-west-1)
  - deployment_mode (hosted|dedicated)  [NOTE: private out of scope for beta]
  - adapter (s3|splunk_hec|datadog_logs)
  - outcome (success|error|dropped|skipped)
  - error_class (auth|throttle|network|timeout|bad_request|server_error|unknown)
  - http_status (optional; bucket into {2xx,4xx,5xx} preferred over full code)
  - reason (small controlled vocabulary only)
  - pause_reason (manual|max_lag|backpressure|circuit_breaker)
- Avoid / prohibit high-card labels:
  - tenant_id, workspace_id, user_id, destination_id, config_id, request_id, event_id
  - raw endpoint/host, full URL, Splunk index/sourcetype, S3 bucket name, Datadog tags
- If per-destination visibility is required:
  - Prefer sampling via logs/traces or a separate internal-only, rate-limited “status endpoint” query.
  - If we must label destinations, use destination_scope=beta_cohort and destination_hash (short, stable, low volume) behind an internal metrics pipeline, not default production Prometheus.

Definitions
- Export lag: time between audit event.created_at and successful delivery acknowledgement at destination adapter.
- Checkpoint age: how “stale” the exporter’s persisted checkpoint is (wall time since last checkpoint write).
- Delivery success rate: successful batches / attempted batches (rolling window), computed at dashboard level.

Inventory table (v0.9)
Columns: Metric | Type | Unit | Description | Labels (allowed) | Cardinality risk | Dashboard panels | Notes / Implementation

1) audit_exporter_export_lag_seconds
- Type: histogram
- Unit: seconds
- Description: End-to-end lag from event timestamp to destination ack (measured on successful delivery only).
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Export lag p50/p90/p99; Lag burn-down
- Notes: Ensure histogram buckets cover 1s -> 24h (beta outages can create multi-hour lag). Proposed buckets: 1,2,5,10,30,60,120,300,600,1800,3600,21600,86400.

2) audit_exporter_lag_max_seconds
- Type: gauge
- Unit: seconds
- Description: Current max observed lag in last scrape interval (or last N minutes) per adapter.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: “Max lag” single-stat per adapter; paging threshold overlay
- Notes: Useful for alerts; avoids heavy quantile queries if histograms are expensive.

3) audit_exporter_events_received_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: events
- Description: Audit events pulled/consumed by exporter before filtering.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Ingest rate; compare to exported
- Notes: Source may be queue/stream consumer; doesn’t mean eligible for export.

4) audit_exporter_events_exported_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: events
- Description: Audit events successfully exported (acked by adapter).
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Export throughput; success ratio vs received
- Notes: Count on ack, not on send.

5) audit_exporter_events_dropped_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: events
- Description: Events dropped by filtering engine or redaction policy enforcement.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, reason
- Cardinality risk: Medium (reason must be controlled)
- Dashboard panels: Dropped events over time; top drop reasons
- Notes: reason vocabulary (beta): filter_no_match | pii_policy_violation | malformed_event | schema_version_unsupported.

6) audit_exporter_batches_attempted_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: batches
- Description: Number of batches attempted to a destination adapter.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Delivery attempts; denominator for success rate
- Notes: Batch definition differs per adapter (Splunk payload, Datadog intake payload, S3 object write).

7) audit_exporter_batches_succeeded_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: batches
- Description: Batches successfully delivered and checkpointed.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Delivery success rate; success trend
- Notes: Increment only after checkpoint commit to maintain “at-least-once” semantics.

8) audit_exporter_batch_size_events
- Type: histogram
- Unit: events
- Description: Distribution of batch sizes (events per batch).
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Batch size p50/p90; tuning view
- Notes: Helps tune rate limiting/backpressure and payload sizing.

9) audit_exporter_adapter_request_duration_seconds
- Type: histogram
- Unit: seconds
- Description: Adapter end-to-end request time (includes retries? preferred: per attempt).
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter, outcome
- Cardinality risk: Medium
- Dashboard panels: Adapter latency p95; correlation with lag spikes
- Notes: outcome vocabulary: success|error|timeout.

10) audit_exporter_adapter_requests_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: requests
- Description: Total adapter send attempts (including retries).
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter, outcome
- Cardinality risk: Medium
- Dashboard panels: Requests/sec; error rate overlay
- Notes: outcome values should be low-card only.

11) audit_exporter_adapter_errors_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: errors
- Description: Adapter errors by class.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter, error_class
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Error breakdown by class; “auth errors” single-stat
- Notes: error_class mapping guidance:
  - Splunk 401/403 => auth
  - Splunk 429 => throttle
  - Datadog 403 => auth (often key scope)
  - network/DNS => network
  - request timeouts => timeout
  - 4xx non-auth/throttle => bad_request
  - 5xx => server_error

12) audit_exporter_retry_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: retries
- Description: Count of retries performed by exporter, by reason.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter, reason
- Cardinality risk: Medium (reason must be controlled)
- Dashboard panels: Retries/min; retry reason distribution
- Notes: reason vocabulary: throttle | transient_network | timeout | server_error.

13) audit_exporter_backoff_sleep_seconds_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: seconds
- Description: Total time spent sleeping due to backoff (proxy for downstream pain).
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Backoff time trend; correlate with lag
- Notes: If hard to implement, drop for beta.

14) audit_exporter_dlq_messages_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: messages/batches
- Description: Messages/batches moved to dead-letter queue.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter, error_class
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: DLQ volume; DLQ by adapter
- Notes: Should page only when sustained + customer impact.

15) audit_exporter_dlq_depth
- Type: gauge
- Unit: messages/batches
- Description: Current DLQ depth.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: DLQ depth (sparkline); “needs replay” indicator
- Notes: Depth gauge depends on DLQ backing store.

16) audit_exporter_checkpoint_age_seconds
- Type: gauge
- Unit: seconds
- Description: Wall-clock age since last successful checkpoint write.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Checkpoint staleness; “stuck exporter” detector
- Notes: Useful even when lag histogram is sparse.

17) audit_exporter_config_sync_success_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: syncs
- Description: Successful config syncs from control plane.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Config sync success; sync rate
- Notes: Exporter can appear “healthy” but misconfigured; sync signals help.

18) audit_exporter_config_sync_errors_total
- Type: counter
- Unit: errors
- Description: Config sync failures.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, error_class
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Config sync errors; error class breakdown
- Notes: error_class here: auth (to control plane), network, server_error, bad_config.

19) audit_exporter_active_configs
- Type: gauge
- Unit: configs
- Description: Number of active export configurations currently loaded.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Active exports count; compare with beta cohort list
- Notes: Avoid per-tenant breakdown.

20) audit_exporter_paused
- Type: gauge
- Unit: boolean (0/1)
- Description: Whether exporter auto-paused deliveries.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode, adapter, pause_reason
- Cardinality risk: Medium
- Dashboard panels: Pause state; pause reason breakdown
- Notes: pause_reason must be small. This is the “what to do” signal for on-call.

21) audit_exporter_queue_depth
- Type: gauge
- Unit: messages
- Description: Depth of internal queue (or consumer lag) for audit events.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Backpressure view; queue depth vs exported rate
- Notes: Implementation depends on queueing model; may be split by stage.

22) audit_exporter_filter_eval_duration_seconds
- Type: histogram
- Unit: seconds
- Description: Time spent evaluating export filters per event or per batch.
- Labels: region, deployment_mode
- Cardinality risk: Low
- Dashboard panels: Filter performance; regression watch
- Notes: Useful for catching pathological filters; ensure it’s not per-tenant labeled.

23) audit_exporter_build_info
- Type: gauge
- Unit: info
- Description: Build/version info for exporter.
- Labels: version, git_sha, region, deployment_mode
- Cardinality risk: Low (but ensure version/sha are not per pod)
- Dashboard panels: Deploy version table
- Notes: Standardize with existing build_info patterns.

Dashboard panel mapping (Grafana)
Dashboard: “Audit Exporter - Overview (Beta)”
- Row: SLO / Customer impact
  - Export lag (p50/p90/p99) by adapter
  - Max lag by adapter (single-stat)
  - Delivery success rate (batches_succeeded_total / batches_attempted_total) by adapter
  - Pause state (paused=1) with pause_reason
- Row: Throughput + volume
  - Events received/sec vs exported/sec
  - Batch size distribution by adapter
- Row: Errors + retries
  - Adapter errors rate by error_class
  - Retries/min by adapter + reason
  - Backoff sleep seconds (if shipped)
- Row: Reliability tooling
  - DLQ depth + DLQ messages/sec
  - Checkpoint age
  - Config sync errors
- Row: Performance
  - Adapter request duration p95 by adapter + outcome
  - Filter eval duration p95

Dashboard: “Audit Exporter - Adapter deep dive”
- Variables: region, deployment_mode, adapter
- Panels:
  - Requests/sec + error rate
  - Request duration heatmap
  - Errors by class
  - Retries by reason
  - Correlate lag p95 to retries

Alert candidates (align to SLO doc; draft)
- Page (severity: high):
  - Max lag > X minutes for Y minutes (by adapter), where X/Y per beta SLO (Sean/Aisha to confirm)
  - paused=1 sustained > 10m (any adapter) AND events_received_total increasing
- Ticket (severity: medium):
  - adapter_errors_total{error_class="auth"} increasing (auth failures usually need customer action)
  - dlq_depth > 0 for > 30m (beta); create ticket with runbook link
  - config_sync_errors_total increasing for > 15m

Open questions / TODO
- Do we need a “customer visible” vs “internal only” metrics split? Current assumption: everything above is internal.
- Should we compute lag based on audit event timestamp (created_at) vs emitter timestamp (emitted_at) vs exporter receive time? (My preference: created_at; Security to confirm semantics.)
- Splunk HEC: do we want a metric for 429 rate specifically? (Could be error_class=throttle; avoid http_status label.)
- Datadog: 403 key scoping confusion recurring—should we special-case a “dd_key_scope_invalid” reason? (If yes, keep as reason value under auth.)
- S3: do we need multipart upload metrics? Likely too detailed for beta; use adapter request duration + errors.

Notes / references
- Implementation started in audit-log-exporter PR-1098 (metrics scaffolding). Ensure names stay stable; changing metrics breaks dashboards and alert rules.
- Dashboard + alerts shipping tracked in observability-pack PR-392.
- Please keep this doc updated as we learn from beta incidents (e.g., downstream throttling, backpressure tuning).

source.title: Experiment metrics backfill plan (schema change to {experiment_id, variant_id})
source.content:
Context
- We added experiment attribution dimensions to request context + telemetry: {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id} (plus model_id, region) per ADR-048.
- Console “Results” view and guardrails are driven off rollup tables keyed by {route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, window_start}.
- During beta we had a schema change + partial rollout (feature flags + region ramp). As a result:
  - historical windows (and even some recent windows during rollout) are missing experiment_id/variant_id
  - Console results appear stale or “no data” for experiments created before the schema flip
  - support tickets are increasingly about “results not updating” when the underlying traffic clearly existed

Goal
Backfill the minimal set of metrics needed by Console route experiment dashboards/guardrails for a bounded time window after the schema change.

Non-goals (for this backfill)
- Not attempting a full reconstruction of all time-series metrics with full label cardinality.
- Not trying to compute statistical significance.
- Not trying to backfill custom/experimental quality signals (eval score stream) in this job.

What we need to backfill (MVP)
Console requires variant-level rollups per experiment, at 1m + 5m windows (5m for most charts; 1m for guardrail evaluation):
- Latency: p50/p95/p99 end-to-end request latency (and optionally model latency if available)
- Errors: error rate, timeout rate, retry rate
- Throughput: req_count, token_count (input/output if available)
- Cost proxy: total_tokens, billed_tokens (if present) and/or cost_usd_estimate if we have it in the pipeline
- Attribution health: attribution_drop_rate (ratio of requests missing experiment_id/variant_id)

Important: we only backfill the analytics rollup tables used by the Results API (not Prometheus/Mimir series). This is intentional to avoid label explosion and because Console reads from analytics.

Retention / source-of-truth constraints
We have multiple telemetry sources with different retention. This determines how far back we can accurately backfill.

Known/assumed retentions (verify per environment; numbers below reflect current defaults as of Mar 2025):
- OTel traces (full-fidelity spans): 7 days
- OTel metrics (high-cardinality tags are sampled/limited): not reliable for backfill; also label strategy changed (PR-28741)
- Request logs (gateway/router access logs with request_id): 30 days in log store; 90 days in cold storage (slow retrieval)
- Analytics “raw request events” table (if enabled): 14 days (partition TTL)
- Analytics rollups (the target): 13 months

Implication
- Best-quality backfill window is limited to the maximum overlap of:
  1) request-level events containing experiment attribution OR
  2) a joinable key (request_id/trace_id) that can be mapped to experiment assignment
- For >14 days, we will likely have to use access logs + join to assignment debug data (if available) OR accept partial coverage.

Proposed backfill window
- Default: backfill last 14 days from the day we confirm “attribution schema fully deployed” in each region.
- Optional extension: last 30 days if access logs are sufficient quality and join works at scale.
- We should NOT attempt beyond 30 days for beta/GA readiness unless an enterprise customer requires it (cost/benefit is poor and risk of incorrect attribution is high).

Schema/version cutover details (what changed)
- Added experiment_id + variant_id into:
  - request context
  - OTel span attributes
  - analytics ingestion schema
  - rollup grouping keys
- Main breakage modes we observed during the cutover:
  - streaming retries dropped attribution (fixed in PR-28622)
  - missing/low-entropy stickiness key caused skew and inconsistent assignment (fixed in PR-28705 + Console warnings)
  - region-by-region rollout caused mixed populations in rollups (older code paths missing tags)

Backfill strategy (high level)
We will run an offline job that recomputes rollups for a bounded time window using request-level events (preferred) or trace-derived request summaries (fallback), and overwrites rollup partitions.

Option A (preferred): backfill from analytics “request event” stream
Prereq: a request-level fact table exists with request_id and experiment_id/variant_id present for the time window (even if some records are missing).
- Input: request_events partitioned by event_time, containing:
  - route_id
  - experiment_id
  - variant_id
  - model_id
  - region
  - latency_ms
  - status_code / error_class
  - token counts
- Transform: aggregate into rollups_1m and rollups_5m with percentiles
- Output: upsert/overwrite partitions in experiment_variant_rollups_* tables

Option B (fallback): backfill by joining router access logs to assignment decision records
Prereq: we can derive or look up experiment assignment by (request_id or trace_id) + stickiness_key.
- Input 1: access logs w/ request_id, route_id, region, latency, model_id, status
- Input 2: assignment decision logs (or a compact “assignment audit” stream) containing request_id/trace_id -> {experiment_id, variant_id}
- Join: request_id (prefer) else trace_id
- Risk: assignment logs may not be complete for older windows; join might be expensive and drop a lot of rows

Option C (not recommended): try to rehydrate from traces only
- Not feasible beyond 7 days due to retention.
- Also traces may be sampled in high-QPS routes; percentiles become biased.

Decision
Proceed with Option A for a 14-day backfill as the primary plan. Keep Option B as contingency if request_events coverage is incomplete.

Implementation plan (jobs + ownership)
Target platform: analytics batch framework (Dataflow/Spark) writing into ClickHouse analytics cluster (rt_analytics). Exact runner TBD by Mei/Caleb based on existing rollup jobs.

Job 1: backfill_experiment_variant_rollups_5m
- Owner: Mei Lin (implementation), Caleb Johnson (pipeline review)
- Schedule: ad-hoc for backfill window; can be re-run idempotently
- Compute:
  - group by window_start_5m, route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region
  - metrics: req_count, err_count, timeout_count, tokens_in/out, latency_p50/p95/p99
- Output table: experiment_variant_rollups_5m
- Write mode: overwrite partitions [start_ts, end_ts]

Job 2: backfill_experiment_variant_rollups_1m (guardrails)
- Owner: Nadia Rahman (metric definitions/cost), Mei Lin (aggregation)
- Same structure as 5m but 1m windows; fewer dimensions if needed for cost (see Cardinality Controls)

Job 3: compute_attribution_drop_rate
- Owner: Logan Wright
- Definition: for each window/route/experiment/region, compute missing-rate:
  - numerator = requests that were eligible for experiment but missing experiment_id OR variant_id
  - denominator = requests on route during window
- Practical note: “eligible for experiment” is hard to determine historically; for backfill we will use a proxy:
  - if experiment_id is present on any request for a route in a window, treat that route+window as “experiment-active” and compute missing-rate vs total requests for that route+window
  - this will slightly understate drop rate when attribution is 0% (i.e., full outage), but we have separate detection for “variant 0 traffic / no tagged traffic”

Partitioning + idempotency
- All output tables are partitioned by date (YYYY-MM-DD). Backfill will overwrite date partitions.
- We need guardrails to prevent overwriting “good” new data with older incorrect logic:
  - store backfill_job_version + backfill_run_id in a sidecar audit table
  - only overwrite partitions within an explicit allowlisted [start_date, end_date]

Cardinality controls (avoid cost blow-ups)
- Per PR-28741 we are limiting tags to prevent high cardinality.
- For backfill tables we must keep the exact dimensions the Results API expects. For anything extra, do NOT add new group-by keys.
- Proposed dimension set for backfill rollups:
  - required: route_id, experiment_id, variant_id
  - strongly preferred: region
  - optional: model_id (only if model_id cardinality is bounded; if not, roll up at “effective_model_family” or omit model_id for backfill)
- Action item: confirm Console queries. If Console requires model-level drill-down for GA, we keep model_id; otherwise we can omit it to keep costs manageable.

Compute cost estimate (rough)
Assumptions (to validate):
- Backfill window: 14 days
- Avg request_events volume: 800M req/day (hosted + dedicated combined), but only a small fraction are on experiment-enabled routes
- Eligible experiment traffic: ~2–5% of total requests during beta (spiky)

Estimate:
- Worst-case rows scanned if we scan full request_events: 11.2B rows
- Mitigation: filter early by route_id in experiment-enabled set (from experiment config service snapshots)
- Expected rows scanned after filter: ~200–600M rows total

We should implement route_id filtering using a daily snapshot of active experiment routes:
- input: experiment_config_snapshots (date, route_id, experiment_id)
- filter: only request_events where route_id in snapshot

Validation plan (must-have)
We will not ship the backfill without data quality checks, because incorrect backfills are worse than “missing data.”

Checks to run per region:
1) Row count sanity
- For each experiment_id and day:
  - sum(req_count across variants) ~= total req_count on route (within expected drift)

2) Split sanity (allocation consistency)
- For fixed split experiments (e.g., 90/10): compare observed variant req_count ratio to configured allocation
- Alert if deviation > 3% absolute over 24h (excluding low-traffic experiments)

3) Percentile sanity
- p50 <= p95 <= p99
- latency percentiles within reasonable bounds vs baseline route dashboards (non-attributed)

4) Join integrity (if we use Option B)
- join success rate >= 99% on request_id path; otherwise abort and reassess

5) Console spot-check
- Pick 2–3 beta accounts (Acme AI, Northbridge) and compare:
  - “No data” -> becomes populated
  - trends match operator expectations from Grafana guardrail dashboards

Operational rollout steps
1) Freeze window
- Choose [start_date, end_date] per region based on deployment completion (feature flag fully enabled + attribution bugfixes deployed).
- Avoid including time where we know streaming attribution was broken (pre PR-28622) unless we accept partial drop.

2) Dry run
- Run jobs in “write to staging tables” mode:
  - experiment_variant_rollups_5m_staging
  - experiment_variant_rollups_1m_staging
- Run validation queries; get sign-off from Observability + Console backend (Chloe) + SRE (Rafael)

3) Production overwrite
- Overwrite partitions in prod rollups
- Monitor:
  - query latency for Results API
  - ClickHouse cluster load
  - attribution_drop_rate time series

4) Post-backfill cleanup
- Drop staging tables after 7 days
- Record run_id + metadata in audit table

Risks / open questions
1) Data gaps due to missing attribution
- If experiment_id/variant_id were missing at ingestion time, we cannot reconstruct without a joinable assignment record.
- If request_events has missing fields but we still have request_id and a deterministic re-eval is possible, we could theoretically re-evaluate assignment offline. BUT:
  - requires exact historical config version + precedence rules + stickiness key used
  - very easy to get wrong; will not do for this backfill

2) Streaming/retry paths
- Pre-fix windows can undercount experiment traffic or misattribute. For those windows, backfill won’t “fix” it.
- Recommendation: choose start_date after PR-28622 is fully deployed to all regions.

3) Region mixing / config propagation lag
- If the same experiment had different configs in different regions, and traffic is region-pinned, we’re ok as long as we group by region.
- If customers use multi-region active-active and assignment is expected to be consistent across regions, we need to be careful interpreting combined results.

4) Cost and cluster impact
- Backfills can saturate ClickHouse merges and impact interactive Console queries.
- Mitigation: throttle writes, run in off-peak UTC windows, set max_threads, and coordinate with SRE.

5) Privacy/compliance
- Ensure we do not backfill any PII fields. The rollups are aggregated; request-level access is only in the batch job and should remain in restricted datasets.

6) Definition drift
- If metric definitions changed (latency definition, token accounting), historical backfills could be inconsistent.
- For this backfill, we will use the same aggregation logic as the current rollup job (pin to commit/tag) and write job_version.

Action items
- [Logan] Confirm which table is canonical “request_events” for hosted/dedicated and whether it retains experiment_id/variant_id for the full 14 days (by 2025-03-25).
- [Mei/Caleb] Prototype filtered scan by route_id snapshot and produce cost estimate for 14-day window (by 2025-03-26).
- [Nadia] Confirm cost/token fields available in request_events; if missing, define the minimal cost proxy for Console (by 2025-03-26).
- [Allison] Confirm required dimensions for Console queries (model_id yes/no) and align with cardinality policy (by 2025-03-27).
- [Rafael] Approve runtime window + throttling plan; add temporary alerting for ClickHouse load during backfill (by 2025-03-28).

Proposed schedule
- Week of Mar 24:
  - confirm data sources + implement staging backfill
  - validation queries and Console spot-check
- Week of Mar 31:
  - production backfill for 14 days (region by region)
  - monitor and adjust

Notes to self / parking lot
- If we later need >30 days for enterprise evidence, consider:
  - a lightweight “assignment decision event” stream with longer retention specifically to enable reconstruction
  - storing daily experiment route snapshots + config versions explicitly for offline replay
- Add a clear customer-facing note in docs: “Results dashboard only guaranteed from <date> forward” unless we commit to systematic long-retention request facts.

Backfill the minimal set of metrics needed by Console route experiment dashboards/guardrails for a bounded time window after the schema change.

Non-goals (for this backfill)
- Not attempting a full reconstruction of all time-series metrics with full label cardinality.
- Not trying to compute statistical significance.
- Not trying to backfill custom/experimental quality signals (eval score stream) in this job.

What we need to backfill (MVP)
Console requires variant-level rollups per experiment, at 1m + 5m windows (5m for most charts; 1m for guardrail evaluation):
- Latency: p50/p95/p99 end-to-end request latency (and optionally model latency if available)
- Errors: error rate, timeout rate, retry rate
- Throughput: req_count, token_count (input/output if available)
- Cost proxy: total_tokens, billed_tokens (if present) and/or cost_usd_estimate if we have it in the pipeline
- Attribution health: attribution_drop_rate (ratio of requests missing experiment_id/variant_id)

Important: we only backfill the analytics rollup tables used by the Results API (not Prometheus/Mimir series). This is intentional to avoid label explosion and because Console reads from analytics.

Retention / source-of-truth constraints
We have multiple telemetry sources with different retention. This determines how far back we can accurately backfill.

Known/assumed retentions (verify per environment; numbers below reflect current defaults as of Mar 2025):
- OTel traces (full-fidelity spans): 7 days
- OTel metrics (high-cardinality tags are sampled/limited): not reliable for backfill; also label strategy changed (PR-28741)
- Request logs (gateway/router access logs with request_id): 30 days in log store; 90 days in cold storage (slow retrieval)
- Analytics “raw request events” table (if enabled): 14 days (partition TTL)
- Analytics rollups (the target): 13 months

Implication
- Best-quality backfill window is limited to the maximum overlap of:
  1) request-level events containing experiment attribution OR
  2) a joinable key (request_id/trace_id) that can be mapped to experiment assignment
- For >14 days, we will likely have to use access logs + join to assignment debug data (if available) OR accept partial coverage.

Proposed backfill window
- Default: backfill last 14 days from the day we confirm “attribution schema fully deployed” in each region.
- Optional extension: last 30 days if access logs are sufficient quality and join works at scale.
- We should NOT attempt beyond 30 days for beta/GA readiness unless an enterprise customer requires it (cost/benefit is poor and risk of incorrect attribution is high).

Schema/version cutover details (what changed)
- Added experiment_id + variant_id into:
  - request context
  - OTel span attributes
  - analytics ingestion schema
  - rollup grouping keys
- Main breakage modes we observed during the cutover:
  - streaming retries dropped attribution (fixed in PR-28622)
  - missing/low-entropy stickiness key caused skew and inconsistent assignment (fixed in PR-28705 + Console warnings)
  - region-by-region rollout caused mixed populations in rollups (older code paths missing tags)

Backfill strategy (high level)
We will run an offline job that recomputes rollups for a bounded time window using request-level events (preferred) or trace-derived request summaries (fallback), and overwrites rollup partitions.

Option A (preferred): backfill from analytics “request event” stream
Prereq: a request-level fact table exists with request_id and experiment_id/variant_id present for the time window (even if some records are missing).
- Input: request_events partitioned by event_time, containing:
  - route_id
  - experiment_id
  - variant_id
  - model_id
  - region
  - latency_ms
  - status_code / error_class
  - token counts
- Transform: aggregate into rollups_1m and rollups_5m with percentiles
- Output: upsert/overwrite partitions in experiment_variant_rollups_* tables

Option B (fallback): backfill by joining router access logs to assignment decision records
Prereq: we can derive or look up experiment assignment by (request_id or trace_id) + stickiness_key.
- Input 1: access logs w/ request_id, route_id, region, latency, model_id, status
- Input 2: assignment decision logs (or a compact “assignment audit” stream) containing request_id/trace_id -> {experiment_id, variant_id}
- Join: request_id (prefer) else trace_id
- Risk: assignment logs may not be complete for older windows; join might be expensive and drop a lot of rows

Option C (not recommended): try to rehydrate from traces only
- Not feasible beyond 7 days due to retention.
- Also traces may be sampled in high-QPS routes; percentiles become biased.

Decision
Proceed with Option A for a 14-day backfill as the primary plan. Keep Option B as contingency if request_events coverage is incomplete.

Implementation plan (jobs + ownership)
Target platform: analytics batch framework (Dataflow/Spark) writing into ClickHouse analytics cluster (rt_analytics). Exact runner TBD by Mei/Caleb based on existing rollup jobs.

Job 1: backfill_experiment_variant_rollups_5m
- Owner: Mei Lin (implementation), Caleb Johnson (pipeline review)
- Schedule: ad-hoc for backfill window; can be re-run idempotently
- Compute:
  - group by window_start_5m, route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, model_id, region
  - metrics: req_count, err_count, timeout_count, tokens_in/out, latency_p50/p95/p99
- Output table: experiment_variant_rollups_5m
- Write mode: overwrite partitions [start_ts, end_ts]

Job 2: backfill_experiment_variant_rollups_1m (guardrails)
- Owner: Nadia Rahman (metric definitions/cost), Mei Lin (aggregation)
- Same structure as 5m but 1m windows; fewer dimensions if needed for cost (see Cardinality Controls)

Job 3: compute_attribution_drop_rate
- Owner: Logan Wright
- Definition: for each window/route/experiment/region, compute missing-rate:
  - numerator = requests that were eligible for experiment but missing experiment_id OR variant_id
  - denominator = requests on route during window
- Practical note: “eligible for experiment” is hard to determine historically; for backfill we will use a proxy:
  - if experiment_id is present on any request for a route in a window, treat that route+window as “experiment-active” and compute missing-rate vs total requests for that route+window
  - this will slightly understate drop rate when attribution is 0% (i.e., full outage), but we have separate detection for “variant 0 traffic / no tagged traffic”

Partitioning + idempotency
- All output tables are partitioned by date (YYYY-MM-DD). Backfill will overwrite date partitions.
- We need guardrails to prevent overwriting “good” new data with older incorrect logic:
  - store backfill_job_version + backfill_run_id in a sidecar audit table
  - only overwrite partitions within an explicit allowlisted [start_date, end_date]

Cardinality controls (avoid cost blow-ups)
- Per PR-28741 we are limiting tags to prevent high cardinality.
- For backfill tables we must keep the exact dimensions the Results API expects. For anything extra, do NOT add new group-by keys.
- Proposed dimension set for backfill rollups:
  - required: route_id, experiment_id, variant_id
  - strongly preferred: region
  - optional: model_id (only if model_id cardinality is bounded; if not, roll up at “effective_model_family” or omit model_id for backfill)
- Action item: confirm Console queries. If Console requires model-level drill-down for GA, we keep model_id; otherwise we can omit it to keep costs manageable.

Compute cost estimate (rough)
Assumptions (to validate):
- Backfill window: 14 days
- Avg request_events volume: 800M req/day (hosted + dedicated combined), but only a small fraction are on experiment-enabled routes
- Eligible experiment traffic: ~2–5% of total requests during beta (spiky)

Estimate:
- Worst-case rows scanned if we scan full request_events: 11.2B rows
- Mitigation: filter early by route_id in experiment-enabled set (from experiment config service snapshots)
- Expected rows scanned after filter: ~200–600M rows total

We should implement route_id filtering using a daily snapshot of active experiment routes:
- input: experiment_config_snapshots (date, route_id, experiment_id)
- filter: only request_events where route_id in snapshot

Validation plan (must-have)
We will not ship the backfill without data quality checks, because incorrect backfills are worse than “missing data.”

Checks to run per region:
1) Row count sanity
- For each experiment_id and day:
  - sum(req_count across variants) ~= total req_count on route (within expected drift)

2) Split sanity (allocation consistency)
- For fixed split experiments (e.g., 90/10): compare observed variant req_count ratio to configured allocation
- Alert if deviation > 3% absolute over 24h (excluding low-traffic experiments)

3) Percentile sanity
- p50 <= p95 <= p99
- latency percentiles within reasonable bounds vs baseline route dashboards (non-attributed)

4) Join integrity (if we use Option B)
- join success rate >= 99% on request_id path; otherwise abort and reassess

5) Console spot-check
- Pick 2–3 beta accounts (Acme AI, Northbridge) and compare:
  - “No data” -> becomes populated
  - trends match operator expectations from Grafana guardrail dashboards

Operational rollout steps
1) Freeze window
- Choose [start_date, end_date] per region based on deployment completion (feature flag fully enabled + attribution bugfixes deployed).
- Avoid including time where we know streaming attribution was broken (pre PR-28622) unless we accept partial drop.

2) Dry run
- Run jobs in “write to staging tables” mode:
  - experiment_variant_rollups_5m_staging
  - experiment_variant_rollups_1m_staging
- Run validation queries; get sign-off from Observability + Console backend (Chloe) + SRE (Rafael)

3) Production overwrite
- Overwrite partitions in prod rollups
- Monitor:
  - query latency for Results API
  - ClickHouse cluster load
  - attribution_drop_rate time series

4) Post-backfill cleanup
- Drop staging tables after 7 days
- Record run_id + metadata in audit table

Risks / open questions
1) Data gaps due to missing attribution
- If experiment_id/variant_id were missing at ingestion time, we cannot reconstruct without a joinable assignment record.
- If request_events has missing fields but we still have request_id and a deterministic re-eval is possible, we could theoretically re-evaluate assignment offline. BUT:
  - requires exact historical config version + precedence rules + stickiness key used
  - very easy to get wrong; will not do for this backfill

2) Streaming/retry paths
- Pre-fix windows can undercount experiment traffic or misattribute. For those windows, backfill won’t “fix” it.
- Recommendation: choose start_date after PR-28622 is fully deployed to all regions.

3) Region mixing / config propagation lag
- If the same experiment had different configs in different regions, and traffic is region-pinned, we’re ok as long as we group by region.
- If customers use multi-region active-active and assignment is expected to be consistent across regions, we need to be careful interpreting combined results.

4) Cost and cluster impact
- Backfills can saturate ClickHouse merges and impact interactive Console queries.
- Mitigation: throttle writes, run in off-peak UTC windows, set max_threads, and coordinate with SRE.

5) Privacy/compliance
- Ensure we do not backfill any PII fields. The rollups are aggregated; request-level access is only in the batch job and should remain in restricted datasets.

6) Definition drift
- If metric definitions changed (latency definition, token accounting), historical backfills could be inconsistent.
- For this backfill, we will use the same aggregation logic as the current rollup job (pin to commit/tag) and write job_version.

Action items
- [Logan] Confirm which table is canonical “request_events” for hosted/dedicated and whether it retains experiment_id/variant_id for the full 14 days (by 2025-03-25).
- [Mei/Caleb] Prototype filtered scan by route_id snapshot and produce cost estimate for 14-day window (by 2025-03-26).
- [Nadia] Confirm cost/token fields available in request_events; if missing, define the minimal cost proxy for Console (by 2025-03-26).
- [Allison] Confirm required dimensions for Console queries (model_id yes/no) and align with cardinality policy (by 2025-03-27).
- [Rafael] Approve runtime window + throttling plan; add temporary alerting for ClickHouse load during backfill (by 2025-03-28).

Proposed schedule
- Week of Mar 24:
  - confirm data sources + implement staging backfill
  - validation queries and Console spot-check
- Week of Mar 31:
  - production backfill for 14 days (region by region)
  - monitor and adjust

Notes to self / parking lot
- If we later need >30 days for enterprise evidence, consider:
  - a lightweight “assignment decision event” stream with longer retention specifically to enable reconstruction
  - storing daily experiment route snapshots + config versions explicitly for offline replay
- Add a clear customer-facing note in docs: “Results dashboard only guaranteed from <date> forward” unless we commit to systematic long-retention request facts.

source.title: Route experiments: attribution drop rate analysis (staging)
source.content:
Last updated: 2025-03-28 (Logan)
Scope: staging only. Goal is to quantify “attribution drop rate” for route-level experiments and identify root causes + fixes before wider beta.

---
Definitions

Attribution fields (expected on request span + metrics events):
- route_id (normalized template)
- experiment_id
- variant_id
- model_id
- region

Attribution drop rate (ADR for this doc; not to be confused with Architecture Decision Record):
- For a given population of requests that SHOULD be eligible for experiment attribution (i.e., route has an active experiment AND request passed experiment evaluation), the % of request records in telemetry that are missing experiment_id OR variant_id.
- Computation uses OTel spans as source of truth for “request seen”; results table uses aggregator view (analytics rollup) for consistency with Console.

Eligibility notes:
- Some traffic is legitimately not attributed (control routes, no experiment active, or policy engine returns “no experiment”). That traffic is excluded.
- Retries count as separate request spans unless we explicitly link them; the question is whether retries retain attribution fields.

---
High-level summary

1) Staging showed meaningful attribution gaps early in Phase 1/2, especially for streaming + retry paths.
2) Most common causes observed:
   a) Missing/low-entropy stickiness keys -> evaluator takes default path and variant assignment differs or is not recorded consistently.
   b) Async span creation / context propagation bugs -> experiment fields not present on child spans or emitted metrics.
   c) Streaming retries -> second attempt missing experiment context (confirmed; fixed in pr-28622).
   d) Gateway header stripping in a subset of staging routes (internal proxy) -> stickiness key header not forwarded.
3) After fixes + instrumentation hardening, drop rate is now near our staging target (<0.5%) for non-streaming; streaming is improved but still elevated in a small subset of routes.

Proposed staging acceptance gate (suggested):
- Non-streaming: attribution drop rate <= 0.25% over 24h
- Streaming: attribution drop rate <= 1.0% over 24h
- AND: no single route > 2.0% drop rate sustained for > 30m (to catch localized regressions)

---
Method / data sources

Time windows analyzed:
- 2025-02-20 to 2025-03-27 (daily slices + spot checks during deploy windows)

Sources:
- OTel traces (request span): presence of experiment_id/variant_id
- Metrics pipeline tags (request-level counters + latency histograms): experiment_id/variant_id tags present
- Analytics rollup table (used by Console results API): join coverage for {route, experiment_id, variant_id}

Notes:
- We intentionally do NOT tag every subspan with experiment_id/variant_id to limit cardinality; this analysis focuses on request span + canonical emitted metrics.
- When tag restrictions/sampling are active (see pr-28741), we use the “canonical request span attributes” as the authoritative check.

---
Sheet 1: Overall staging drop rates (all eligible traffic)

Columns:
- Window (UTC)
- Eligible requests
- Missing experiment_id
- Missing variant_id
- Missing either (drop rate)
- Streaming share
- Top suspected driver

| Window (UTC)        | Eligible req | Missing exp_id | Missing var_id | Missing either | Drop rate | Streaming share | Top suspected driver |
|---------------------|--------------|----------------|----------------|----------------|----------:|----------------:|----------------------|
| 2025-02-20 (24h)    | 1,184,220    | 9,421          | 10,037         | 13,882         | 1.17%     | 28%             | Context propagation gaps (async spans) |

- Logan: propose attribution SLO + alert thresholds for staging + beta (draft by 2025-04-02)
- Caleb: instrumentation change proposal for async context propagation (design doc snippet by 2025-04-03)
- Vikram/Amara: verify header forwarding on internal proxies + document guidance for Private/VPC ingress (by 2025-04-05)
- Allison/Rafael: add runbook section “attribution drop investigation” and link from alerts (by 2025-04-08)

- eligible_requests = spans where route_id in (experiment routes) AND experiment_evaluated=true
- dropped = eligible_requests where (experiment_id is null OR variant_id is null)
- drop_rate = dropped / eligible_requests
