source.title: Experiment metrics taxonomy (working notes) — route-level A/B testing
source.body:
Context
- Route-level experiments need first-class metrics for guardrails + results views.
- This doc is a working taxonomy to align:
  (1) what we emit (OTel spans/metrics + logs)
  (2) what we aggregate (rollups for Console)
  (3) what we alert on (SRE guardrails)
- We are optimizing for: correctness, stable definitions, and avoiding metric cardinality blow-ups.

Non-goals (for now)
- No full stat-sig engine (Console will show deltas + guardrail status only).
- No customer custom metrics ingestion.
- No per-user breakdowns beyond deterministic assignment.

1) Canonical attribution dimensions (labels/attributes)
We need a single canonical set of dimensions across telemetry + rollups.

Required dimensions (request-scoped)
- route_id (normalized, templated route; NOT raw path)
- experiment_id (stable UUID / opaque id)
- variant_id (stable id within experiment config; do not use display name)
- model_id (resolved final model actually served, after routing)
- region (e.g., us-east, eu-west)

Strongly recommended dimensions (but may be sampled / restricted)
- deployment_mode (hosted | dedicated | private) — helps interpret baselines
- customer_tier (free | pro | enterprise) — useful for incident triage and rate-limit behavior
- http_status / rpc_status (already present in many spans)

Avoid / explicitly not tagging in high-volume metrics
- customer_id (too high cardinality)
- sticky_key (PII / high cardinality)
- request_id (obviously)
- raw_route (raw path can explode cardinality)

Notes
- If experiment_id is absent, metrics must still emit with experiment_id="" (empty) rather than omitting the attribute entirely, to keep queries consistent.
- For “control” traffic not in experiment, experiment_id="" and variant_id="".

2) Route ID normalization
Goal: stable “route_id” that maps to what Console shows and avoids cardinality issues.

Decision (tentative, align with platform routing)
- route_id is the configured route template id from policy engine / router config.
- Example:
  - Good: route_id="chat.completions.v1" or "/v1/chat/completions"
  - Bad: "/v1/chat/completions?user=123" or "/v1/chat/completions/acct_9f…"

Pitfall
- If we use “raw path” we will DOS our metrics backend (and make dashboards unusable).

Open question
- Do we need a second field route_path_template for human readability vs internal id? (If yes: only in logs/analytics, not on hot-path metrics.)

3) Latency taxonomy
We need to disambiguate “latency” because streaming + retries + queuing can produce confusing numbers.

Request-level latencies (primary for guardrails)
A. end_to_end_latency_ms
- Definition: from request accepted at Redwood edge/gateway to terminal response completion.
- For streaming: “completion” means last chunk flushed (not first token).
- Includes: routing, queueing, model compute, streaming, retries (if same logical request).
- Use: error budgets / SLO-ish; less useful for model perf root cause.

B. time_to_first_token_ms (TTFT)
- Definition: from request accepted to first streamed token emitted.
- Applies only to streaming endpoints.
- For non-streaming, omit or report as null in analytics.
- Use: UX / perceived latency; useful for caching/batching regression detection.

C. queue_wait_ms
- Definition: time from request accepted to beginning of model execution on GPU.
- Source: router/runtime internal span timing.
- Use: capacity issues, propagation lag side-effects, batching/caching behavior.

D. model_compute_ms
- Definition: model execution time (prefill + decode) excluding queue.
- Use: kernel/model change evaluation; ties more directly to cost.

Token-level latencies (secondary; expensive)
E. prefill_ms, decode_ms
- Only emit as span attributes; do not emit as high-cardinality timeseries by {route, experiment, variant} unless sampled.
- Reason: per-variant x per-model x per-region x per-bucket creates too many series.

Percentiles
- Guardrails + dashboards will display p50/p95/p99 for:
  - end_to_end_latency_ms
  - time_to_first_token_ms (streaming)
- For p99: be careful with small sample sizes (see Sampling section).

Units
- ms everywhere.

Known pitfalls / edge cases
- Retries:
  - If a request is retried internally, we need to decide whether end_to_end includes both attempts. Current recommendation: yes, as experienced by caller.
  - But we also need attempt_count to debug and isolate “retry storms”.
- Streaming disconnects:
  - If client disconnects mid-stream, end_to_end is truncated and can look “good”. Need a flag: client_cancelled=true.

4) Error taxonomy
We need consistent “error rate” definition across services.

Primary error rate
- error_rate = count(request_outcome="error") / count(request_outcome in {"success","error"})
- request_outcome is determined at gateway (final).

Breakdowns (for drill-down)
- error_class
  - "client" (4xx)
  - "server" (5xx)
  - "timeout" (deadline exceeded)
  - "capacity" (explicit overload / shed)
  - "policy" (blocked by policy / authz)
- error_code
  - stable internal codes where possible (avoid raw exception strings)

Important
- Don’t treat “stream interrupted by client” as server error; track separately as client_cancelled.

5) Throughput taxonomy
Used mainly for interpreting guardrails and diagnosing skew.

Metrics
- requests_per_minute (RPM)
- tokens_in_per_minute, tokens_out_per_minute
- tokens_total_per_minute = tokens_in + tokens_out

Caveat
- Tokens are only known after tokenization; for streaming, tokens_out might arrive incrementally. Ensure final counts are emitted on request completion.

6) Cost attribution taxonomy
We want “cost per output token” and related metrics that are stable and explainable.

Two parallel notions of “cost”
A) Resource cost (engineering truth)
- gpu_ms (or accelerator_ms)
- cpu_ms (optional)
- memory_gb_s (not in v1)
- cache_hit indicators (prefix_cache_hit, kv_cache_reuse) as categorical attributes

B) Estimated $ cost (product-facing)
- estimated_cost_usd
  - derived from gpu_ms * region/hardware rate card + overhead factor
  - must be versioned: cost_model_version

Console-facing cost metrics (v1)
- cost_per_1k_output_tokens_usd
  - estimated_cost_usd / (tokens_out / 1000)
- cost_per_request_usd
  - estimated_cost_usd

Rules
- If tokens_out == 0 (errors, empty responses), do not compute cost_per_token; show null and keep cost_per_request.
- Include retries in cost (since it’s real resource usage).

Pitfalls
- Cross-model comparisons: different models may have different tokenization; tokens_out is not apples-to-apples. Keep “gpu_ms per output token” as an internal debugging metric.
- Rate card changes: historical comparisons need cost_model_version and potentially backfill/recompute.

7) Sampling + cardinality controls
We need a strict plan to avoid an observability bill explosion.

Cardinality risk dimensions
- experiment_id x variant_id x route_id x model_id x region is already large.
- Add customer_id / prompt template / sticky key and it becomes unbounded.

Proposed strategy (aligning with PR-28741)
1) Always-on low-cardinality metrics (hot path)
- Emit request_count, error_count, end_to_end_latency_ms histogram with dimensions:
  - route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region
- Do NOT include model_id on always-on metrics unless the route is pinned to a single model.
  - Alternative: include model_family (low cardinality) if available.

2) Sampled “expanded” metrics (debug)
- For N% of traffic (configurable, default 1%), emit additional attributes/dimensions:
  - model_id, deployment_mode, attempt_count, cache flags
- Sampling must be consistent across spans + metrics so joins are possible.

3) Logs for rare deep debugging
- Full assignment explanation should go to logs/debug endpoint (internal), not metrics.

Guardrail alerting implications
- Alerts based on p99 with small sample sizes are noisy.
- Recommendation: guardrails should include a min_request_count threshold before activating p99-based auto-stop.

8) Aggregation windows + rollups (Console)
Console requires fast queries and stable numbers; raw OTel queries are too expensive.

Near-real-time rollups (for dashboards)
- 1m rollup table keyed by:
  - time_bucket_1m, route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region
- Fields:
  - request_count
  - error_count
  - latency_histogram (or precomputed p50/p95/p99 approximations)
  - tokens_in_sum, tokens_out_sum
  - estimated_cost_usd_sum

Console default views
- 5m and 1h views should be computed as rollups of 1m buckets.
- For “experiment lifetime” view: aggregate from experiment start_time to now using rollups.

Data freshness SLO (tentative)
- Rollup availability lag p95 < 3 minutes for active experiments.
- Beyond 10 minutes is “degraded” and should show banner in Console.

9) Metric naming + OTel conventions (draft)
We need consistent names; below are placeholders pending final naming review.

OTel span attributes (recommended)
- redwood.route_id
- redwood.experiment_id
- redwood.variant_id
- redwood.region
- redwood.model_id (sampled)
- redwood.request_outcome (success|error)
- redwood.error_class (optional)
- redwood.client_cancelled (bool)

OTel metrics (timeseries)
- redwood.inference.request.count (counter)
- redwood.inference.request.latency_ms (histogram)
- redwood.inference.request.ttft_ms (histogram, streaming only)
- redwood.inference.tokens.in (counter)
- redwood.inference.tokens.out (counter)
- redwood.inference.cost.estimated_usd (counter)

Histogram buckets
- Need to align with existing global latency buckets to keep backend efficient.
- TTFT needs separate bucket distribution (typically smaller than end-to-end for long streams, but can spike under queueing).

10) Known issues observed so far (staging/beta)
- Attribution drop on streaming retries:
  - experiment_id/variant_id missing in downstream spans when retry path re-creates request context.
  - Action: ensure request context persists through retry wrapper (see bugfix references).
- Variant skew when stickiness key missing:
  - default key was low-entropy (e.g., empty string) leading to non-uniform distribution.
  - Mitigation: validation + warnings; fallback to derived stable key only when safe.
- Propagation lag hiding in metrics:
  - experiments started in control plane but not applied in router for several minutes; metrics showed “no change” and confused users.
  - Need: config_propagation_lag_seconds metric and banner in Console.
- Cost per token NaNs/inf:
  - errors with zero tokens_out causing divide-by-zero in rollup job.
  - Fix: explicit null handling.

11) TODOs / decisions needed
- Confirm final low-cardinality dimension set for always-on metrics (do we include model_id or not?).
- Decide how to represent “fallback served a different model than variant intended”:
  - Proposed: variant_intent_model_id vs served_model_id (served_model_id is the truth; intent only for debugging).
- Define minimum sample size rules for guardrails in Console:
  - e.g., do not evaluate p99 guardrails until >= 500 requests per variant in last 15m.
- Confirm if we want per-route default guardrails vs per-experiment overrides (PM decision impacts required fields in rollups).

Appendix A: Quick glossary
- Route experiment: experiment defined at routing layer; variants typically map to model/policy differences.
- Variant: a branch within an experiment (A/B/n).
- Guardrail: threshold condition (latency, error, cost, quality) that triggers warnings / auto-stop.

Appendix B: Example query patterns (for humans)
- “Is variant B slower than A?”
  - Compare p95 end_to_end_latency_ms by {experiment_id, variant_id} over last 1h.
- “Is skew happening?”
  - Compare request_count per variant vs expected allocation; alert if absolute delta > X% for Y minutes.
- “Is this a capacity problem?”
  - Check queue_wait_ms distribution + shed errors; correlate with region.

- Use: error budgets / SLO-ish; less useful for model perf root cause.

B. time_to_first_token_ms (TTFT)
- Definition: from request accepted to first streamed token emitted.
- Applies only to streaming endpoints.
- For non-streaming, omit or report as null in analytics.
- Use: UX / perceived latency; useful for caching/batching regression detection.

C. queue_wait_ms
- Definition: time from request accepted to beginning of model execution on GPU.
- Source: router/runtime internal span timing.
- Use: capacity issues, propagation lag side-effects, batching/caching behavior.

D. model_compute_ms
- Definition: model execution time (prefill + decode) excluding queue.
- Use: kernel/model change evaluation; ties more directly to cost.

Token-level latencies (secondary; expensive)
E. prefill_ms, decode_ms
- Only emit as span attributes; do not emit as high-cardinality timeseries by {route, experiment, variant} unless sampled.
- Reason: per-variant x per-model x per-region x per-bucket creates too many series.

Percentiles
- Guardrails + dashboards will display p50/p95/p99 for:
  - end_to_end_latency_ms
  - time_to_first_token_ms (streaming)
- For p99: be careful with small sample sizes (see Sampling section).

Units
- ms everywhere.

Known pitfalls / edge cases
- Retries:
  - If a request is retried internally, we need to decide whether end_to_end includes both attempts. Current recommendation: yes, as experienced by caller.
  - But we also need attempt_count to debug and isolate “retry storms”.
- Streaming disconnects:
  - If client disconnects mid-stream, end_to_end is truncated and can look “good”. Need a flag: client_cancelled=true.

4) Error taxonomy
We need consistent “error rate” definition across services.

Primary error rate
- error_rate = count(request_outcome="error") / count(request_outcome in {"success","error"})
- request_outcome is determined at gateway (final).

Breakdowns (for drill-down)
- error_class
  - "client" (4xx)
  - "server" (5xx)
  - "timeout" (deadline exceeded)
  - "capacity" (explicit overload / shed)
  - "policy" (blocked by policy / authz)
- error_code
  - stable internal codes where possible (avoid raw exception strings)

Important
- Don’t treat “stream interrupted by client” as server error; track separately as client_cancelled.

5) Throughput taxonomy
Used mainly for interpreting guardrails and diagnosing skew.

Metrics
- requests_per_minute (RPM)
- tokens_in_per_minute, tokens_out_per_minute
- tokens_total_per_minute = tokens_in + tokens_out

Caveat
- Tokens are only known after tokenization; for streaming, tokens_out might arrive incrementally. Ensure final counts are emitted on request completion.

6) Cost attribution taxonomy
We want “cost per output token” and related metrics that are stable and explainable.

Two parallel notions of “cost”
A) Resource cost (engineering truth)
- gpu_ms (or accelerator_ms)
- cpu_ms (optional)
- memory_gb_s (not in v1)
- cache_hit indicators (prefix_cache_hit, kv_cache_reuse) as categorical attributes

B) Estimated $ cost (product-facing)
- estimated_cost_usd
  - derived from gpu_ms * region/hardware rate card + overhead factor
  - must be versioned: cost_model_version

Console-facing cost metrics (v1)
- cost_per_1k_output_tokens_usd
  - estimated_cost_usd / (tokens_out / 1000)
- cost_per_request_usd
  - estimated_cost_usd

Rules
- If tokens_out == 0 (errors, empty responses), do not compute cost_per_token; show null and keep cost_per_request.
- Include retries in cost (since it’s real resource usage).

Pitfalls
- Cross-model comparisons: different models may have different tokenization; tokens_out is not apples-to-apples. Keep “gpu_ms per output token” as an internal debugging metric.
- Rate card changes: historical comparisons need cost_model_version and potentially backfill/recompute.

7) Sampling + cardinality controls
We need a strict plan to avoid an observability bill explosion.

Cardinality risk dimensions
- experiment_id x variant_id x route_id x model_id x region is already large.
- Add customer_id / prompt template / sticky key and it becomes unbounded.

Proposed strategy (aligning with PR-28741)
1) Always-on low-cardinality metrics (hot path)
- Emit request_count, error_count, end_to_end_latency_ms histogram with dimensions:
  - route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region
- Do NOT include model_id on always-on metrics unless the route is pinned to a single model.
  - Alternative: include model_family (low cardinality) if available.

2) Sampled “expanded” metrics (debug)
- For N% of traffic (configurable, default 1%), emit additional attributes/dimensions:
  - model_id, deployment_mode, attempt_count, cache flags
- Sampling must be consistent across spans + metrics so joins are possible.

3) Logs for rare deep debugging
- Full assignment explanation should go to logs/debug endpoint (internal), not metrics.

Guardrail alerting implications
- Alerts based on p99 with small sample sizes are noisy.
- Recommendation: guardrails should include a min_request_count threshold before activating p99-based auto-stop.

8) Aggregation windows + rollups (Console)
Console requires fast queries and stable numbers; raw OTel queries are too expensive.

Near-real-time rollups (for dashboards)
- 1m rollup table keyed by:
  - time_bucket_1m, route_id, experiment_id, variant_id, region
- Fields:
  - request_count
  - error_count
  - latency_histogram (or precomputed p50/p95/p99 approximations)
  - tokens_in_sum, tokens_out_sum
  - estimated_cost_usd_sum

Console default views
- 5m and 1h views should be computed as rollups of 1m buckets.
- For “experiment lifetime” view: aggregate from experiment start_time to now using rollups.

Data freshness SLO (tentative)
- Rollup availability lag p95 < 3 minutes for active experiments.
- Beyond 10 minutes is “degraded” and should show banner in Console.

9) Metric naming + OTel conventions (draft)
We need consistent names; below are placeholders pending final naming review.

OTel span attributes (recommended)
- redwood.route_id
- redwood.experiment_id
- redwood.variant_id
- redwood.region
- redwood.model_id (sampled)
- redwood.request_outcome (success|error)
- redwood.error_class (optional)
- redwood.client_cancelled (bool)

OTel metrics (timeseries)
- redwood.inference.request.count (counter)
- redwood.inference.request.latency_ms (histogram)
- redwood.inference.request.ttft_ms (histogram, streaming only)
- redwood.inference.tokens.in (counter)
- redwood.inference.tokens.out (counter)
- redwood.inference.cost.estimated_usd (counter)

Histogram buckets
- Need to align with existing global latency buckets to keep backend efficient.
- TTFT needs separate bucket distribution (typically smaller than end-to-end for long streams, but can spike under queueing).

10) Known issues observed so far (staging/beta)
- Attribution drop on streaming retries:
  - experiment_id/variant_id missing in downstream spans when retry path re-creates request context.
  - Action: ensure request context persists through retry wrapper (see bugfix references).
- Variant skew when stickiness key missing:
  - default key was low-entropy (e.g., empty string) leading to non-uniform distribution.
  - Mitigation: validation + warnings; fallback to derived stable key only when safe.
- Propagation lag hiding in metrics:
  - experiments started in control plane but not applied in router for several minutes; metrics showed “no change” and confused users.
  - Need: config_propagation_lag_seconds metric and banner in Console.
- Cost per token NaNs/inf:
  - errors with zero tokens_out causing divide-by-zero in rollup job.
  - Fix: explicit null handling.

11) TODOs / decisions needed
- Confirm final low-cardinality dimension set for always-on metrics (do we include model_id or not?).
- Decide how to represent “fallback served a different model than variant intended”:
  - Proposed: variant_intent_model_id vs served_model_id (served_model_id is the truth; intent only for debugging).
- Define minimum sample size rules for guardrails in Console:
  - e.g., do not evaluate p99 guardrails until >= 500 requests per variant in last 15m.
- Confirm if we want per-route default guardrails vs per-experiment overrides (PM decision impacts required fields in rollups).

Appendix A: Quick glossary
- Route experiment: experiment defined at routing layer; variants typically map to model/policy differences.
- Variant: a branch within an experiment (A/B/n).
- Guardrail: threshold condition (latency, error, cost, quality) that triggers warnings / auto-stop.

Appendix B: Example query patterns (for humans)
- “Is variant B slower than A?”
  - Compare p95 end_to_end_latency_ms by {experiment_id, variant_id} over last 1h.
- “Is skew happening?”
  - Compare request_count per variant vs expected allocation; alert if absolute delta > X% for Y minutes.
- “Is this a capacity problem?”
  - Check queue_wait_ms distribution + shed errors; correlate with region.

source.title: Kickoff notes — Internal Unit Economics Dashboard (v1)
source.content:
Meeting: Internal Unit Economics Dashboard kickoff (v1)
Date/time: 2025-01-15 10:00–11:00 PT
Location: Zoom
Facilitator: Talia Benson (PgM)
Notes: Logan Wright

Attendees
- Product: Marissa Cole (Console & Rollouts), Aditya Rao (Optimize) [partial]
- Eng (Telemetry/Analytics): Logan Wright, Nadia Rahman, Mei Lin, Caleb Johnson
- Eng (Console/Tooling): Chloe Martin, Felix Schneider [partial], Nora Ahmed [partial]
- SRE/Obs: Sean Gallagher, Allison Grant
- Finance: Rishi Malhotra (FP&A), Laura Bennett (CFO) [first 20 mins]

Why we’re here (problem statement)
- Today we can answer “what’s our infra spend?” and “what’s our token volume?” but not reliably “what is blended $/1M output tokens by model/region/tier and why did it change?”
- Goal is a finance-aligned metric definition + dependable pipeline + internal Console UI for drilldowns.
- v1 is internal-only; used for exec reporting + cost optimization prioritization.

Stakeholder asks / success criteria (captured live)
Finance (Laura, Rishi)
- Need a single definition of “blended $/1M output tokens” that matches FP&A reporting within an agreed tolerance.
- Must support QTD and month-close reporting (stable numbers, versioned methodology, clear caveats).
- Ability to explain deltas: “why did cost per token move this week?” (even if we can’t attribute perfectly, we need key drivers).
- Reconciliation: dashboard totals vs billing totals vs infra cost sources; track known gaps explicitly.

Product (Marissa, Aditya)
- Drilldowns: model (and model version / quant profile), region, customer tier.
- Provide driver signals for Optimize: cache hit, batching utilization, avg output length, streaming vs non-streaming.
- Detect regressions: if a runtime change causes cost spike for a subset (e.g., eu-west + specific model) we need to see it quickly.

Engineering (Logan/Nadia/Chloe)
- Keep telemetry cardinality under control (quant profile + route IDs can explode).
- Pipeline must have clear ownership/oncall; “dependable enough for exec slides” requires SLOs + alerts.
- Keep query latency low (pre-aggregation where needed).

SRE/Obs (Sean/Allison)
- Define pipeline SLOs (freshness + completeness), alert policy, and runbook.
- Avoid alert noise; prefer “confidence indicator” or coverage checks tied to data quality.

Scope alignment (v1)
In scope
- Canonical definition of blended $/1M output tokens.
- Hourly aggregation job + warehouse views.
- Internal Console page: time series + breakdown tables + basic drilldown navigation.
- Data quality checks + alerts + oncall runbook.

Out of scope (explicit)
- External/customer-facing cost dashboard.
- Fully automated optimization actions (this is measurement + diagnostics).

Initial metric definition (draft v0 — not signed off)
Primary KPI: Blended cost per 1M output tokens
- Name (working): blended_cost_per_1m_output_tokens
- Unit: USD per 1,000,000 output tokens

Definition sketch:
- Denominator: total output tokens emitted (completion tokens), aggregated by hour and dimensions (model, region, tier, etc.).
- Numerator: allocated compute cost (GPU-hours * rate card) + serving overhead allocations (if/when included), mapped to the same hour and dimensions.

Formula (draft):
- blended_cost_per_1m_output_tokens = (allocated_cost_usd / output_tokens) * 1,000,000

Notes/caveats discussed:
- We must be explicit about what is included in “allocated_cost_usd”:
  - GPU compute (core)
  - CPU/egress/storage (maybe later or as overhead bucket)
  - Shared platform overhead (control plane, logging) (likely later; Finance wants a plan)
- Idle capacity treatment:
  - Dedicated/Reserved pools: do we allocate idle to the tier/customer, or spread blended across all tokens?
  - Finance preference: consistent with how capacity commitments are accounted for (likely allocate within the pool/tier; define a separate “utilization” metric).
- Two views proposal (not decided, but strong interest):
  - “Live” view: fastest, near-real-time (for eng triage)
  - “Finance” view: stable, versioned, rate-card-locked for period reporting (QTD / month close)

Dimensions (v1 target)
Required dimensions
- time_bucket: hourly (warehouse partition)
- model_id (canonical), model_version (if available), quantization_profile (allowed set)
- region (canonical normalized label)
- customer_tier: self_serve | dedicated | private | enterprise (definitions TBD)

Nice-to-have (likely v1.1)
- route / product surface (chat vs embeddings vs rerank)
- request_type flags (streaming, tool-calling)
- customer segment (but must avoid customer-identifiable drilldowns in Console)

Identified gaps / known issues (as of kickoff)
1) Streaming output token metering parity
- Current suspicion: streaming responses under-count output tokens in some paths.
- Risk: denominator too low => cost per token looks artificially high and moves unpredictably.
- Plan: audit streaming pipeline, add regression tests, canary checks.

2) Customer tier mapping source-of-truth
- Problem: tier is derived differently across systems (billing plans, contract flags, routing/dedicated pools, private deployments).
- Edge cases: pilots, credits, resellers, mixed-mode customers (hosted + dedicated).
- Need: authoritative derivation logic + backfill strategy.

3) Region normalization
- Problem: region labels differ across gateway/runtime/infra billing inputs (cloud region vs internal colo labels).
- Need: mapping table with “unknown” handling (don’t drop rows), and an ownership + update process.

4) Model identifiers / aliases
- Model names differ across runtime config vs marketing names vs evaluation harness.
- Need canonical model_id + version + quant profile tagging so grouping is stable.

5) Cost inputs and rate card provenance
- Finance wants rate card source, update cadence, and versioning (e.g., by quarter).
- Need to avoid “silent” changes causing time series discontinuities.

6) Data warehouse performance
- Drilldowns by multiple dimensions can be slow if computed on the fly.
- Likely need materialized views by hour + key dimensions; query caching for common views.

SLO / data quality proposals (first pass)
Freshness
- Hourly aggregates available within 90 minutes of hour close (target); 2 hours (max) for SLO.
Completeness
- >= 99% of expected hourly buckets present over trailing 24h.
- Dimension coverage checks:
  - Unknown/NULL region < 0.5% of tokens per hour
  - Unknown/NULL tier < 0.5% of tokens per hour
  - Streaming vs non-streaming split present (no sudden disappearance)
Correctness / drift
- Alert if output tokens drop > X% hour-over-hour for top models/regions absent a matching traffic drop signal.
- Alert if blended cost jumps > Y% for a stable traffic mix (to be tuned; avoid noise).

Operational notes
- Ownership proposal:
  - Telemetry & aggregation: Eng Telemetry/Analytics (Logan/Nadia)
  - Console endpoints + UI: Console & Tooling (Chloe)
  - SLOs/alerts: SRE/Obs (Sean/Allison)
  - Methodology sign-off: Finance (Rishi/Laura)
- Oncall: add a rotation alias for “unit-econ-pipeline” alerts (initially telemetry oncall; SRE consult).

Action items (captured)
A) Metric definition + signoff
- [Rishi + Nadia] Write a 1–2 page methodology note: numerator/denominator rules, idle treatment, overhead policy (include/exclude), and reconciliation approach. Due: 2025-01-24.
- [Talia] Schedule Finance signoff review (30 mins) once draft is ready. Target: week of 2025-01-27.

B) Telemetry gaps
- [Nadia] Audit streaming token metering paths; list code paths missing output token events; propose fix + tests. Target ticket: ENG-8421.
- [Caleb] Add a data quality check specifically for streaming/non-streaming parity (tokens by streaming flag). Target: ENG-8429.

C) Dimension normalization
- [Mei] Draft canonical region mapping table and ingestion plan (incl. unknown handling). Target: ENG-8424.
- [Cole Summers] Confirm GPU cost input fields map cleanly to canonical region + capacity type. Follow-up with infra thread.
- [Quentin Harris] Provide canonical model_id/model_version mapping source and update process. (Need to avoid alias churn.)

D) Customer tier mapping
- [Logan + Chloe] Define tier derivation logic from billing/contract metadata + routing flags; document edge cases + backfill plan. Target: ENG-8423.

E) Aggregation + warehouse
- [Mei] Prototype hourly aggregation schema and views (by model/region/tier) with partitioning and expected query patterns.
- [Logan] Draft dependency graph (metering -> aggregation -> views -> API -> UI) and propose SLAs per stage.

F) Console UI / API
- [Chloe + Felix] Bring rough API contract for time series + breakdown queries (group_by, filters, pagination). Review in #eng thread.
- [Marissa + Alicia] Provide initial filter defaults (Finance vs Eng persona) + empty states + rounding guidelines.

Open questions (parking lot)
1) Idle capacity accounting (Dedicated / Private)
- Do we allocate idle to the tier/pool even if no tokens that hour? If yes, how do we represent hours with zero tokens (infinite cost)?
- Suggestion: allow cost allocation independent of token events; show utilization side-by-side; handle zero-token hours as “no denominator” rather than spiking charts.

2) Overhead inclusion
- v1 likely compute-only; Finance wants a roadmap for overhead buckets and a consistent approach.

3) Timezone/day boundaries
- Finance reports by business day/month close; pipeline is hourly UTC by default. Need clear convention and UI display.

4) Privacy / access control
- Confirm we will not expose customer-level identifiers in Console drilldowns.
- RBAC: internal-only, likely allowlist initially (Finance, Product leads, core eng).

5) Reconciliation target and tolerance
- What is acceptable delta vs billing totals? (e.g., <2% monthly?)
- Need to explicitly list known sources of discrepancy (late arriving events, retries, credits, etc.).

Decisions made in meeting (soft)
- v1 focuses on “blended $/1M output tokens” and drilldowns by model/region/tier.
- Treat streaming output token parity and tier mapping as top risks and prioritize early.
- Plan to operationalize with SLOs + oncall runbook from the start (not after UI ships).

Next meeting
- Weekly working session (30 mins): Telemetry + Finance + Console sync. Owner: Talia.
- First agenda: review methodology draft, confirm dimension list + cardinality guardrails, finalize SLO targets.

Notes to self (Logan)
- Don’t let “metric definition” slip until after pipeline build. We need signoff early or we’ll chase moving targets.
- Make sure “unknown” handling never drops rows silently (region/tier/model) — prefer explicit unknown buckets + coverage indicators.
- Push for a versioned methodology + rate card table keyed by effective_date so Finance can lock periods.

text:
Meeting: Internal Unit Economics Dashboard kickoff (v1)
Date/time: 2025-01-15 10:00–11:00 PT
Location: Zoom
Facilitator: Talia Benson (PgM)
Notes: Logan Wright

Attendees
- Product: Marissa Cole (Console & Rollouts), Aditya Rao (Optimize) [partial]
- Eng (Telemetry/Analytics): Logan Wright, Nadia Rahman, Mei Lin, Caleb Johnson
- Eng (Console/Tooling): Chloe Martin, Felix Schneider [partial], Nora Ahmed [partial]
- SRE/Obs: Sean Gallagher, Allison Grant
- Finance: Rishi Malhotra (FP&A), Laura Bennett (CFO) [first 20 mins]

Why we’re here (problem statement)
- Today we can answer “what’s our infra spend?” and “what’s our token volume?” but not reliably “what is blended $/1M output tokens by model/region/tier and why did it change?”
- Goal is a finance-aligned metric definition + dependable pipeline + internal Console UI for drilldowns.
- v1 is internal-only; used for exec reporting + cost optimization prioritization.

Stakeholder asks / success criteria (captured live)
Finance (Laura, Rishi)
- Need a single definition of “blended $/1M output tokens” that matches FP&A reporting within an agreed tolerance.
- Must support QTD and month-close reporting (stable numbers, versioned methodology, clear caveats).
- Ability to explain deltas: “why did cost per token move this week?” (even if we can’t attribute perfectly, we need key drivers).
- Reconciliation: dashboard totals vs billing totals vs infra cost sources; track known gaps explicitly.

Product (Marissa, Aditya)
- Drilldowns: model (and model version / quant profile), region, customer tier.
- Provide driver signals for Optimize: cache hit, batching utilization, avg output length, streaming vs non-streaming.
- Detect regressions: if a runtime change causes cost spike for a subset (e.g., eu-west + specific model) we need to see it quickly.

Engineering (Logan/Nadia/Chloe)
- Keep telemetry cardinality under control (quant profile + route IDs can explode).
- Pipeline must have clear ownership/oncall; “dependable enough for exec slides” requires SLOs + alerts.
- Keep query latency low (pre-aggregation where needed).

SRE/Obs (Sean/Allison)
- Define pipeline SLOs (freshness + completeness), alert policy, and runbook.
- Avoid alert noise; prefer “confidence indicator” or coverage checks tied to data quality.

Scope alignment (v1)
In scope
- Canonical definition of blended $/1M output tokens.
- Hourly aggregation job + warehouse views.
- Internal Console page: time series + breakdown tables + basic drilldown navigation.
- Data quality checks + alerts + oncall runbook.

Out of scope (explicit)
- External/customer-facing cost dashboard.
- Fully automated optimization actions (this is measurement + diagnostics).

Initial metric definition (draft v0 — not signed off)
Primary KPI: Blended cost per 1M output tokens
- Name (working): blended_cost_per_1m_output_tokens
- Unit: USD per 1,000,000 output tokens

Definition sketch:
- Denominator: total output tokens emitted (completion tokens), aggregated by hour and dimensions (model, region, tier, etc.).
- Numerator: allocated compute cost (GPU-hours * rate card) + serving overhead allocations (if/when included), mapped to the same hour and dimensions.

Formula (draft):
- blended_cost_per_1m_output_tokens = (allocated_cost_usd / output_tokens) * 1,000,000

Notes/caveats discussed:
- We must be explicit about what is included in “allocated_cost_usd”:
  - GPU compute (core)
  - CPU/egress/storage (maybe later or as overhead bucket)
  - Shared platform overhead (control plane, logging) (likely later; Finance wants a plan)
- Idle capacity treatment:
  - Dedicated/Reserved pools: do we allocate idle to the tier/customer, or spread blended across all tokens?
  - Finance preference: consistent with how capacity commitments are accounted for (likely allocate within the pool/tier; define a separate “utilization” metric).
- Two views proposal (not decided, but strong interest):
  - “Live” view: fastest, near-real-time (for eng triage)
  - “Finance” view: stable, versioned, rate-card-locked for period reporting (QTD / month close)

Dimensions (v1 target)
Required dimensions
- time_bucket: hourly (warehouse partition)
- model_id (canonical), model_version (if available), quantization_profile (allowed set)
- region (canonical normalized label)
- customer_tier: self_serve | dedicated | private | enterprise (definitions TBD)

Nice-to-have (likely v1.1)
- route / product surface (chat vs embeddings vs rerank)
- request_type flags (streaming, tool-calling)
- customer segment (but must avoid customer-identifiable drilldowns in Console)

Identified gaps / known issues (as of kickoff)
1) Streaming output token metering parity
- Current suspicion: streaming responses under-count output tokens in some paths.
- Risk: denominator too low => cost per token looks artificially high and moves unpredictably.
- Plan: audit streaming pipeline, add regression tests, canary checks.

2) Customer tier mapping source-of-truth
- Problem: tier is derived differently across systems (billing plans, contract flags, routing/dedicated pools, private deployments).
- Edge cases: pilots, credits, resellers, mixed-mode customers (hosted + dedicated).
- Need: authoritative derivation logic + backfill strategy.

3) Region normalization
- Problem: region labels differ across gateway/runtime/infra billing inputs (cloud region vs internal colo labels).
- Need: mapping table with “unknown” handling (don’t drop rows), and an ownership + update process.

4) Model identifiers / aliases
- Model names differ across runtime config vs marketing names vs evaluation harness.
- Need canonical model_id + version + quant profile tagging so grouping is stable.

5) Cost inputs and rate card provenance
- Finance wants rate card source, update cadence, and versioning (e.g., by quarter).
- Need to avoid “silent” changes causing time series discontinuities.

6) Data warehouse performance
- Drilldowns by multiple dimensions can be slow if computed on the fly.
- Likely need materialized views by hour + key dimensions; query caching for common views.

SLO / data quality proposals (first pass)
Freshness
- Hourly aggregates available within 90 minutes of hour close (target); 2 hours (max) for SLO.
Completeness
- >= 99% of expected hourly buckets present over trailing 24h.
- Dimension coverage checks:
  - Unknown/NULL region < 0.5% of tokens per hour
  - Unknown/NULL tier < 0.5% of tokens per hour
  - Streaming vs non-streaming split present (no sudden disappearance)
Correctness / drift
- Alert if output tokens drop > X% hour-over-hour for top models/regions absent a matching traffic drop signal.
- Alert if blended cost jumps > Y% for a stable traffic mix (to be tuned; avoid noise).

Operational notes
- Ownership proposal:
  - Telemetry & aggregation: Eng Telemetry/Analytics (Logan/Nadia)
  - Console endpoints + UI: Console & Tooling (Chloe)
  - SLOs/alerts: SRE/Obs (Sean/Allison)
  - Methodology sign-off: Finance (Rishi/Laura)
- Oncall: add a rotation alias for “unit-econ-pipeline” alerts (initially telemetry oncall; SRE consult).

Action items (captured)
A) Metric definition + signoff
- [Rishi + Nadia] Write a 1–2 page methodology note: numerator/denominator rules, idle treatment, overhead policy (include/exclude), and reconciliation approach. Due: 2025-01-24.
- [Talia] Schedule Finance signoff review (30 mins) once draft is ready. Target: week of 2025-01-27.

B) Telemetry gaps
- [Nadia] Audit streaming token metering paths; list code paths missing output token events; propose fix + tests. Target ticket: ENG-8421.
- [Caleb] Add a data quality check specifically for streaming/non-streaming parity (tokens by streaming flag). Target: ENG-8429.

C) Dimension normalization
- [Mei] Draft canonical region mapping table and ingestion plan (incl. unknown handling). Target: ENG-8424.
- [Cole Summers] Confirm GPU cost input fields map cleanly to canonical region + capacity type. Follow-up with infra thread.
- [Quentin Harris] Provide canonical model_id/model_version mapping source and update process. (Need to avoid alias churn.)

D) Customer tier mapping
- [Logan + Chloe] Define tier derivation logic from billing/contract metadata + routing flags; document edge cases + backfill plan. Target: ENG-8423.

E) Aggregation + warehouse
- [Mei] Prototype hourly aggregation schema and views (by model/region/tier) with partitioning and expected query patterns.
- [Logan] Draft dependency graph (metering -> aggregation -> views -> API -> UI) and propose SLAs per stage.

F) Console UI / API
- [Chloe + Felix] Bring rough API contract for time series + breakdown queries (group_by, filters, pagination). Review in #eng thread.
- [Marissa + Alicia] Provide initial filter defaults (Finance vs Eng persona) + empty states + rounding guidelines.

Open questions (parking lot)
1) Idle capacity accounting (Dedicated / Private)
- Do we allocate idle to the tier/pool even if no tokens that hour? If yes, how do we represent hours with zero tokens (infinite cost)?
- Suggestion: allow cost allocation independent of token events; show utilization side-by-side; handle zero-token hours as “no denominator” rather than spiking charts.

2) Overhead inclusion
- v1 likely compute-only; Finance wants a roadmap for overhead buckets and a consistent approach.

3) Timezone/day boundaries
- Finance reports by business day/month close; pipeline is hourly UTC by default. Need clear convention and UI display.

4) Privacy / access control
- Confirm we will not expose customer-level identifiers in Console drilldowns.
- RBAC: internal-only, likely allowlist initially (Finance, Product leads, core eng).

5) Reconciliation target and tolerance
- What is acceptable delta vs billing totals? (e.g., <2% monthly?)
- Need to explicitly list known sources of discrepancy (late arriving events, retries, credits, etc.).

Decisions made in meeting (soft)
- v1 focuses on “blended $/1M output tokens” and drilldowns by model/region/tier.
- Treat streaming output token parity and tier mapping as top risks and prioritize early.
- Plan to operationalize with SLOs + oncall runbook from the start (not after UI ships).

Next meeting
- Weekly working session (30 mins): Telemetry + Finance + Console sync. Owner: Talia.
- First agenda: review methodology draft, confirm dimension list + cardinality guardrails, finalize SLO targets.

Notes to self (Logan)
- Don’t let “metric definition” slip until after pipeline build. We need signoff early or we’ll chase moving targets.
- Make sure “unknown” handling never drops rows silently (region/tier/model) — prefer explicit unknown buckets + coverage indicators.
- Push for a versioned methodology + rate card table keyed by effective_date so Finance can lock periods.
